I believe that, in this case, the usual review approach is unfit for purpose. I see that you intend to release this as `36.0.0.rc1`. Indeed, I suggest that we introduce for this case, both as a way to unblock and as an experiment for future similar cases, a prerelease system. Could we publish this as `36.0.0-rc1` (with a dash, not a dot, to abide by [SemVer§9](https://semver.org/#spec-item-9) and try it out / ask for it to be tried out on several country packages? 🙂 If no issues arise, then we'll publish as `36.0.0`! 😃 If there are issues, then we'll iterate and fix them.

Hi Maverick, thank you for implementing this! I made some comments. In addition to the comments Simon made, I have a couple main suggestions 
1) I think you could simplify this by creating an instance of EPL instead of EPLMajorAxis, and using the routines in EPL to compute lensing quantities. Then, you can use the multipole class to compute the lensing quantities for the multipole term, and then just add the outputs.  
2) it looks like a rotated multipole model is being added to an un-rotated epl model, you might want to check that this gives the correct behavior. I think this will result in the two profiles being misaligned by the angle phi_G.

Thank you for your draft! The task it much trickier that I initially have expected. I gave it some thought (and recalled some `crud` behavior) and I think that we should try to rework some things. I'll summarize my comments here.
- Try to get rid of class `Crud`.
- Try to work with `call`s only.
- Do not expose configuration handles.
- Raise errors as exceptions.
- Optimize utils usage.

I see that the first one is a requirement to be able to use `conn.crud.method`, but is such approach really widespread in Python? I'm not well-experienced myself, but I think that I have seen providing many handles from a single place more often than using complex hierarchy. Maybe it's worth to study this question a little by using some Python modules as an example (and if you still decide to use `conn.crud.method`, maybe you'll see some nice implementation approaches).

It is highly likely that I have forgot to mention something important in this review, so feel free to ask me any additional questions here or in personal chat.

First of all, so exciting to see you back around here @dmontagu! 🤩🎉

## PR Review

Now to the PR review:

I've been reviewing this for the last couple of days. Very clever all around. Amazing job, as always! 🚀

I added a couple of comments here and there. A couple of opinions in the places where I suspect my opinion could be relevant.

## Type Checks from VS Code - PyRight

I noticed a couple of errors with VS Code/PyRight in some places, I think mainly about getting a dict key form a dict where it is not required, or things like that.

Would it be valuable to point them out and suggest the fixes or is it just too much noise on top of the actual PR review?

I'm just adding a couple of those comments for now.

## Questions

About some of the questions in the description, my opinions inline.

Pretty much in favor with everything, for the rest I don't have a strong opinion or enough knowledge.

**Things left to resolve:**

* [x] Field kwargs
	* [x] Should we remove or keep the 'examples' kwarg on `Field`?
		* If not, what is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Re-add the examples keyword argument
			* [@tiangolo]: In favor with plan. All things equal, +1 to re-adding it. Unless there's something against it.
	* [x] Should we remove or keep the 'const' kwarg on `Field`? (This was used to indicate a field was required and the only allowed value was its default.)
		* If not, what is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Drop this argument in favor of `Literal` and/or validators as appropriate (i.e., keep as is on main branch)
			* [@tiangolo]: In favor with plan. If it's the same, I would think it's better to keep one single way to do it, and if there's now a "standard" way to do it with type annotations, I would use that. One single way reduces combinatorial logic, maintenance, testing. And it's easier for users to learn one single thing than two, even more if it's two competing ones.
	* [x] Should we change the `regex` kwarg to `pattern` in the `Field` signature?
		* **Tentative plan:** Change `regex` to `pattern` (i.e., keep as is on main branch)
			* [@tiangolo]: In favor with plan. As JSON Schema uses the same `pattern` keyword, I would vote for that. `regex` might be more intuitive from Python-land, but for me, `regex` is more ambiguous as to if it takes a regular expression object or a pattern string. Even though the Python docs also refer to a Pattern object, but anyway, I personally associate the word "pattern" more easily with the string.
	* [x] Do we want to do a backwards-compatibility handler thing with `**kwargs` — warning about now-invalid kwargs (whether or not we keep them working) on `Field`?
		* **Tentative plan:** Do not attempt to maintain backwards compatibility, but document in the migration guide
			* [@tiangolo] **Strongly** in favor with plan. More details below, and also inline on the PR.


* [ ] ConfigDict fields
	* [ ] Do we want to re-add 'schema_extra' as a field on model_config?
		* If not, should we drop the tests?
		* Either way, do we retain/drop support on a config _class_ in the model definition?
		* What is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Drop in favor of `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan.
	* [ ] Do we want to re-add 'fields' as a field on model_config?
		* If not, should we drop the tests?
		* What is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Drop in favor of annotations and/or `= Field(...)`
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan.

* [ ] Default values
	* If a default value of a field is not JSON encodable, do we include anything in the schema indicating the default value? Do we error?
	* **Tentative plan:** Just don't include the value in the schema if json encoding fails, but keep the `required` field set appropriately. I believe this is perfectly compatible with JSON schema.
		* [@tiangolo] Soft in favor with plan. I'm not sure about the implications, but I think it should be fine.

* [ ] `__modify_schema__` (-> `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`)
	* Do we retain backwards compatibility with the name `__modify_schema__`, or force migration to `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`?
		* **Tentative plan:** Force the migration to `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan. This is the moment to break changes.
	* Do we support any args/kwargs for `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__` besides the schema itself?
		* Do we pass any info to it from FieldInfo? The CoreSchema? A "source" object? (Could be useful when working with custom generics)
		* **Tentative plan:** Not sure. Would potentially be useful to have access to the core schema and to the type "source" (e.g., to check generic parameter values, etc.)

* [ ] FastAPI
	* Do we need to retain functions analogous to any of the following? (Note we have retained `model_schema`, and `schema`.)
		* get_flat_models_from_field/get_flat_models_from_fields
		* get_flat_models_from_model/get_flat_models_from_models
		* get_model_name_map
		* model_process_schema
	* If not, can we drop their tests?
	* **Tentative plan:** Could we instead build a CoreSchema in FastAPI that produces the desired JSON schema?
		* I'm inclined to put significant effort into helping with the migration of FastAPI to pydantic v2, and to implement any necessary analogous functions as a part of that effort. But if it's already clear that we will need them, happy to add them now.
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan. And thanks, as always! 🎉 Better not to add premature optimizations/abstractions. And more details in line in the PR.

* [ ] Callable type
	* How should we handle JSON schema generation for fields with type callable? (See, e.g., `tests.test_schema.test_callable_type`)
	* **Tentative plan:** Not sure.

* [ ] Decimal
	* What should the Decimal schema be?
	* **Tentative plan:** Need to come to a consensus; https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/1511 may be relevant
		* Note: although serialization and parsing are different, I think for FastAPI/OpenAPI purposes it is important that the JSON schema be compatible with the serialization type. (Or else generated clients will produce type errors if an API returns a Decimal.) Happy to discuss this more.
			* [@tiangolo] agreed that incompatible clients would be a non-obvious but important problem. Although I'm not sure I understand the problem well and what's the "serialization type" in this context.

## kwargs in `Field()`

Details about asking for no more kwargs in `Field()`:

I would request to **not** accept any extra kwargs. I asked for that here in this hidden comment: https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/4516#discussion_r1010870226

Copying here for context/completeness:

> This `**extra` has been used to add stuff to JSON Schema.

> But in some cases, people pass other stuff here expecting it to be used later or for other purposes, but then it also affects JSON Schema.

> I would like to ask to make it a single keyword argument `extra` instead of `**extra`, or alternatively, to have another single argument `json_schema_extra` so that extensions for JSON Schema can be apart from extra values passed here for other purposes.

> I haven't seen a use for this apart from JSON Schema, and the fact that it makes `Field()` accept any keyword arguments makes editors not complain about arguments that actually do nothing. I've seen this sometimes, e.g. recently with a `Field(required=True)`, but `required` is not a valid argument, if it's required or not is defined by the absence of a `default`. But the code seemed fine and linters passed, because it was taken by the catch all `**extra`.

> It's a breaking change, but I think it's totally worth it and this is the right time for those changes.

## Semi-off topic, highlights

**Note**: no action item here, just praise and highlights of non-obvious extra benefits. 🎉

Very clever implementation! 🚀

I like how the core schema refs, def refs, and json refs are stored and correlated, and then updated when necessary, but still stored. And how everything is stored in the object.

I think this will allow me to do something I've been wanting to do in FastAPI, to pre-generate the JSON Schema stuff for each endpoint, and when there's an error, to show the specific endpoint with an error instead of just saying "something is wrong".

## Wishlist - collect errors

I was checking the wishlist of things I wanted for Pydantic and almost everything is already done. 🎉

From the things not there yet, there's one single thing I would like to ask that is related to JSON Schema, although not sure if it could be a subsequent PR. It's also related to the point above.

### The problem

First the problem.

Currently, an example like this, rightfully breaks:

```Pyhton
from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict


class Car:
    def __init__(self, make: str, model: str, year: int):
        self.make = make
        self.model = model
        self.year = year

class Model(BaseModel):
    f1: int = 1
    f2: Car

    model_config = ConfigDict(arbitrary_types_allowed=True)


Model.model_json_schema()
```

But if `Model` is in a FastAPI path operation, and maybe `Car` is in a sub-model in a sub-field, in a large app, when calling the OpenAPI endpoint the whole thing will break, saying there's a problem with `Car`, but without saying where exactly... and more importantly, none of the OpenAPI will be generated. So, a single broken field breaks the whole OpenAPI and Swagger UI.

### Feature Request

Currently it immediately raises an error like:

```
Cannot generate a JsonSchema for X
```

...that stops the rest of the JSON Schema generation.

I would like instead to accumulate those errors and get them all together, but to be able to retrieve the rest of the JSON Schema.

### Possible Implementation

I think it's better to state the problem in case you see a better way to solve it, but I can also imagine possible implementations. For example:

```Python
schema = Model.model_json_schema()
```

...raises as normally, but:

```Python
schema, errors = Model.model_json_schema(keep_errors=True)
```

Doesn't raise, and returns a JSON Schema where anything with an error is replaced with `{}` and `errors` is a list of stored exceptions. Of course, `keep_errors` could use some bikeshedding on the parameter name, the same for the return interface, but you get the idea.

It could also be a context manager, maybe too complex, but that would keep the API interface (params, return) of `model_json_schema()` unmodified for both cases, don't know. And it would probably only be used by FastAPI and libraries like that, so not too bad on that side. But it would require contextvars and all that dance. E.g.

```Python
with ErrorCollector as c:
	schema = Model.model_json_schema()
print(e.errors)
```

Again, the specific implementation is up for discussion, but I would like to have a way to do this.

### Plan in FastAPI

My plan is also later to put this in FastAPI in a way that (if I manage to) when there's an error like this, the traceback would use Rich and point to the right file and line with the specific FastAPI *path operation* that uses that model. But I think that to be able to do that I first need to pre-compute and collect the JSON Schema errors per path-operation, and not only at the global level.

## Breaking Changes

About the breaking changes, mostly renaming things, in the small few places this happens, I think this is the right moment to do it and discard the cruft of backwards compatibility for those things.

An improvement in that migration, alternative to keep backwards compatibility, would be a tool to automatically update code to use the new things, for the simple ones (I can imagine, e.g. moving kwargs in `Field()` to `json_schema_extra`, or changing `regex` to `pattern`). Maybe that could also work for the `model_config`.

I haven't done it, but I know it's possible and feasible with LibCST. @Kludex has built a couple of CLI tools that do that type of thing, e.g. https://github.com/Kludex/bump-testclient

I'm with @joshmoore, that this example:
```python
z = zarr.array(a, chunks=10, meta_array=cupy.empty(()), compressor=CuPyCPUCompressor(Zlib()))
```
looks a little odd.

I'm wondering if we should make more of a distinction between 
* the compressor specification (the goal of how bytes should look like in the store) and
* the compressor implementation (the path of how to make and read those bytes specifically)

When thinking of what I'd want to express in this line, I'd say
> I want to put my array `a` onto a store (which is implicitly `dict` in this case), in chunks of 10 and it should be zlib-compressed.

I don't really care about how the data moves from `a` to the store (if a is a `numpy` array, I'd probably like to use `numcodecs` directly, if `a` is a `cupy` array, I'd like to use GDS and a GPU compressor if available and like to fall back to numcodecs if not). In that sense, the line should maybe read like `z = zarr.array(a, chunks=10, compressor=Zlib())`.

Afterwards, I'd probably want to get a selection back from the zarr-array `z`, and I might want that to live on the GPU (which **might be a different choice** from what `a` was...), this creates a bit of a problem. I might want to write something like:

```python
selection: cupy.ndarray = z[3:7]
```
... but Python doesnt't (yet?) work this way, so we need to pass the type-information differently into `z`. It might be
```python
selection = z.get(slice(3,7), meta_array=cupy.empty(()))
```
which is far less beautiful than
```python
selection = z[3:7]
```
For the last option to work, there must be a way to change the default return type of the `[]`-operator, so passing the `meta_array` into `zarr.array` additionally might be a viable option. Still something like the `get` might be useful for some, if the array might be used both on CPU and GPU.

In any case, up to now, the `CuPyCPUCompressor` (which I consider as a description of the path between array and store) would not be visible at any place, and it might also be different for different set / get calls of the same zarr array `z`. It might be given as an optional hint on creation of the array, if a specific path is desired. Probably even with a way of overriding it on set or get. In any case, I believe that having any implementation (path) -specific information stored in zarray metadata would be a bad thing. In particular, I'd argue that `cupy_cpu_compressor` should maybe not appear in `.zarray` metadata.

The TimeSeries behavior is a bit more nuanced than just reloading upon "changePlugin to timeseries".  I think it avoids loading data if no reload occurred in the time while the plugin was inactive.

The Time Series Effect has an extra condition on changePlugin, to check "tagLoadState === DataLoadState.NOT_LOADED".  Importantly, its Reducer makes sure to set the loaded flag back to `NOT_LOADED` when a reload occurs [1].  In this way, it avoids reloading more often than needed.  This behavior is not really obvious because it is split across the Effect + Reducer.

Ideally, I think we would just make Debugger have the same behavior.  However, since the Debugger doesn't seem to have much attention atm, adding a TODO would also suffice for me.  wdyt?

[1] https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard/blob/83574eb51248d5db0a29b65684e185ff533c7e2a/tensorboard/webapp/metrics/store/metrics_reducers.ts#L341

IMO, this is a good idea since this is a library and should be as flexible as possible, only blocking dependencies that are _known_ to not work. Consider this table:
| | Compatible | Incompatible |
| --- | ---| --- |
| `~=` | Users cannot try running with 4.x, even though it would work fine. | Users cannot try running with 4.x, but it probably wouldn't work anyways (though, it might because they may not use incompatible features). |
| `>=` | Users can run with 4.x seamlessly. | Users can try running with 4.x, but it may not work. |

The bottom left and top right show the best case scenario for each operator, while the top left and the bottom right show the worst case, and we can see `>=` outperforming in both cases, primarily because it leaves the user/dependent/downstream in control of their own destiny.

Hi @2xmm,

First of all, thanks for taking the time to submit this clearly documented + well-tested contribution, and improving some of the adjacent code along the way. I'm tempted to suggest making the default behavior for the library fetching JSON instead of CSV given the significant performance implications, but never know who may be depending on the presence of timezones in their datetime strings. 

I have 3 followup comments for you:

1. Would you like to be added to the [AUTHORS.rst](https://github.com/hydrosquall/tiingo-python/blob/master/AUTHORS.rst) file? You can provide your preferred name / email, whichever you're comfortable with.
2. To the [comment](https://github.com/hydrosquall/tiingo-python/issues/523#issuecomment-711080213) about the YAML files looking different: is this referring to how the arrays all seem to be broken out onto newlines instead  remaining on the line of their associated key? E.g.

```yaml
Response-Type: [*]

# vs

Response-Type
  - '*"
```

If so, my guess would be that perhaps an text editor plugin is may be reformatting the YAML on save, depending on whether you used the helper python script or direct file edits to remove your API keys. Were there any other differences that stood out to you?

In any event, the Travis CI tests passing indicates to me that the alternate syntax is still valid YAML, so I think this is fine, but thanks for pointing this out. A future TODO for this project may be to add a consistency linter like `black` and run that as a pre-commit hook to remove this sort of variation.

3. Do you have any suggestions about where to document the presence of this new parameter? I was thinking that adding a few lines to the `get_dataframe` code block in `README.rst` could be a good starting point, indicating both the option and the performance benefit + tradeoff (e.g. no timezones in your date field). (If this feels out of scope, I'll come up with something before we cut a new version.

Once again, great work! Thanks for sharing your fix with the world, I'm excited to bring this new capability into the library.

3. An integration test in `certbot-ci/certbot_integration_tests/certbot_tests/test_main.py`, to check that the feature works end-to-end and that the resulting certificate has the expected expiry date. In the future, when you open a PR or issue that comes from some previous discussion, please link that discussion. That helps anyone who finds this PR from search to find all of the relevant context, and helps reviewers who might not have been part of that original conversation to learn what they need to give an effective review.

In this case, I'm linking https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/issues/3829#issuecomment-811115417.

<hr>

You don't have to close and re-open PRs on GitHub to re-trigger CI. In most projects, you can just push an empty commit. In LightGBM we have a slight preference for that because a new commit means new builds, which means it is possible to view the logs from the previous builds, which can be useful in debugging. Some CI services overwrite builds if you close and re-open. This is why, for example, only the most recent build of this PR is currently available on GitHub Actions, even though there have been two.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7608904/113497173-f2085580-94c6-11eb-9cf1-8ff5e7249a1a.png)

You can push an empty commit like this:

```shell
git commit --allow-empty -m "empty commit"
git push tests/cluster-fixture
```

### Fixes

Just found this bug as well -- I think this should be a `<=` in the condition. That's why this sort is happening so often.

https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/eb23df0ce0b3a7e9ff1bd746f2944559fea206a9/python/ray/tune/execution/tune_controller.py#L208-L213

### Suggestions

Can we add comments in the constructor for `_stopping_actors`, describing what this map is, who populates it, and who is responsible for cleaning it up. Will help the next person out (and me in the future 😄)!

```python
        # This tracks actors that have been requested to stop.
        # Maps a tracked actor to the timestamp of the stop request.
        # Adding:
        # - ...
        # Removing:
        # - `on_stop` callback that gets called by the actor manager once the actor has actually stopped
        # - Forcefully cleaned up by `_cleanup_stopping_actors(force=True)` at the end of execution during cleanup
        # - Periodically cleaned up by `_cleanup_stopping_actors(force=False)` after reaching the `_actor_cleanup_timeout`.
        self._stopping_actors: Dict[TrackedActor, float] = {}
```

### Clarification questions

- The reason why we have these pending actors is to enable autoscaling? For a fixed cluster, they will just be pending forever, so we'll clean their metadata in the actor manager.
- Is it possible to remove `stopping_actors` from the `TuneController` and just handle it in the actor manager? If an actor has been stopping for longer than the timeout, just kill it. Then, we don't have to keep this extra state in the controller.

I'm a bit fussy about patching framework fundamentals, and especially about touching header casing. Manipulating header casing keeps causing us incredible headaches, so I would prefer not to make this broad of a patch. Moreover, technically the `Headers` does not allow boolean values in the first place.

It seems better to fix the root of the problem, which seems to be here in moto: https://github.com/spulec/moto/blob/3118090fdc38de64e0a7aef969b3b1992c1703f5/moto/s3/models.py#L248-L250 I think we should just change the line to `str(self.bucket_key_enabled).lower()` there.

Moving forward this shouldn't be a problem at all, since we are currently migrating s3 to the new framework, which will serialize the response types properly into an HTTP response, which covers boolean to string conversions. But for now, it seems moto is the best place to make this change.
